1:
"And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying," The witnesses note God commanding the war on Midian after the plague of Peor and shortly before Moses' death. The witnesses note that Gill sets it after the plague upon Israel for the fornication and idolatry into which the daughters of Moab and Midian had drawn them, and a little before the death of Moses. The witnesses note that Henry notes the LORD of hosts giving orders to make war upon the Midianites. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the LORD spake — commanding the war on Midian; the after the plague — the seduction at Peor avenged; the before Moses' death — his last great service; the God's justice — his accounts settled in his season.

The witnesses agree the war was a charge laid on Moses as his last great service: the LORD of hosts commanded vengeance on Midian for the seduction at Peor, and Moses, soon to die, was to see it done before he was gathered to his people; the timing shows that God settles his accounts in his own season, and that the leader's closing labour was to execute the divine justice on those who had wrought such ruin among the people of God.

So the witnesses make the verse the command given: The witnesses note the the LORD spake: commanding the war on Midian. The witnesses note the after the plague: the seduction at Peor avenged. The witnesses note the before Moses' death: his last great service. The witnesses note the God's justice: his accounts settled in his season. The expositors observe the the LORD spake: commanding the war on Midian. The expositors observe the after the plague: the seduction at Peor avenged. The expositors observe the before Moses' death: his last great service. The expositors observe the God's justice: his accounts settled in his season. The believer is taught to mark the the LORD spake, the after the plague, the before Moses' death, the God's justice, and to reverence the holiness and justice of God. The verse thus records that God commanded the war on Midian after Peor — Moses' last great service, the LORD settling his account with those who had ruined his people.
2:
"Avenge the children of Israel of the Midianites: afterward shalt thou be gathered unto thy people." The witnesses note God bidding Moses avenge Israel on Midian, after which he should die. The witnesses note that Gill notes the vengeance was for the injury done by sending their daughters to entice Israel to uncleanness and idolatry, for which 24,000 perished; the Moabites, though concerned, were spared (for Ruth's sake, some think). The witnesses note that afterward Moses should be gathered to his people. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the avenge the children — for the seduction at Peor; the the Midianites — who drew Israel into sin; the Moab spared — though concerned in the matter; the gathered to thy people — moses to die when the work was done.

The witnesses agree the vengeance was just and the time of it merciful to Moses: Midian had deliberately seduced Israel into the sin that cost twenty-four thousand lives, and God required their punishment; yet he linked it with Moses' death — "afterward shalt thou be gathered" — that the faithful servant might finish this work before he rested, the execution of justice his last act, and his death a going home when his task was done.

So the witnesses make the verse avenge, then die: The witnesses note the avenge the children: for the seduction at Peor. The witnesses note the the Midianites: who drew Israel into sin. The witnesses note the Moab spared: though concerned in the matter. The witnesses note the gathered to thy people: Moses to die when the work was done. The expositors observe the avenge the children: for the seduction at Peor. The expositors observe the the Midianites: who drew Israel into sin. The expositors observe the Moab spared: though concerned in the matter. The expositors observe the gathered to thy people: Moses to die when the work was done. The believer is taught to mark the avenge the children, the the Midianites, the Moab spared, the gathered to thy people, and to reverence the holiness and justice of God. The verse thus records that Moses was to avenge Israel on Midian and then die — the vengeance just for the seduction at Peor, his last work finished before his going home.

The witnesses note Moses summoning the people to war, the vengeance being the LORD's own quarrel. The witnesses note that Gill notes he spake in obedience to the divine command, to the heads or princes of the tribes. The witnesses note that Clarke stresses it was God's quarrel, not their own — the Midianites were idolaters, idolatry an offence against God, and the civil power has no authority to meddle with what belongs to him without special command. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the arm yourselves — moses summoning the warriors; the to avenge the LORD — his quarrel, not Israel's; the against idolaters — the offence against God himself; the by express command — israel but the instrument of his judgment.

The Wesleyan reader (Clarke) marks the ground of the war: it was "to avenge the LORD of Midian," his quarrel and not Israel's, the punishment of idolaters being God's right, which men may execute only by his express command; the distinction guards the war from being a thing of human passion or ambition — it was a divine commission, the LORD himself the avenger, and Israel but the appointed instrument of his judgment.

So the witnesses make the verse the LORD's quarrel: The witnesses note the arm yourselves: Moses summoning the warriors. The witnesses note the to avenge the LORD: his quarrel, not Israel's. The witnesses agree the equal levy from every tribe made the war the act of the whole nation: each tribe sent its thousand, that all Israel might share in avenging the LORD and none stand apart from the common cause; the modest number — twelve thousand against a numerous people — also prepared the way for God's glory in the victory, that the triumph might plainly be his and not the strength of a great host.

8:
"And they slew the kings of Midian... five kings of Midian: Balaam also the son of Beor they slew with the sword." The witnesses note the five kings of Midian slain, and Balaam the son of Beor falling by the sword. The witnesses note that Gill notes the five kings were petty kings or princes, perhaps under the king of Moab; Keil and Delitzsch name them. The witnesses note that Balaam, who had counselled the seduction, was found among the Midianites and slain. The witnesses agree the death of Balaam is the most pointed note of the battle: the false prophet who could not curse Israel but taught Balak to seduce them, and who loved the wages of unrighteousness, was found among the very people he had counselled and perished by the sword with them; his end is the apostles' warning fulfilled — that he "loved the wages of unrighteousness" (2 Pet. 2:15) and went in the way of his own gain (Jude 11), the covetous deceiver reaping at last the destruction he had wrought.

The witnesses agree the taking of the captives and the spoil set the stage for what followed: the warriors brought back the women, the children, and the wealth of Midian — yet the sparing of the women, who had been the very instruments of Israel's seduction, would presently draw Moses' anger; the verse records the gathering of the prey, the occasion of the stern command to come, that the snare which had ruined Israel not be carried home alive.

The witnesses note that Gill notes the spoil of the cities and castles, and the prey of men and beasts. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the all the spoil and prey — of men and beasts; the brought to Moses — not seized for themselves; the disinterestedness — the rare self-restraint of the victors; the submitting to God — the spoils owned as his to divide.

The reformed reader (Calvin) commends the soldiers' self-restraint: instead of seizing the spoil for themselves, as victorious armies are wont, they brought the whole of it to Moses and Eleazar, owning that it was not theirs to dispose of but God's, to be divided as he should appoint; the modesty and disinterestedness of the warriors, bringing home the prey to the appointed authority, is a rare and commendable thing, the fruit of a heart that seeks not its own but submits the spoils of victory to God.
